t: Wilson G.T. et al.'s meta-analysis indicated a significant effect in BED.
9. — FDA approval for BED; significantly reduces binge-eating frequency (McElroy S.L. JAMA Psychiatry 2015).
SSRI — for comorbid MDD; modest effect on binge eating frequency.
Weight management — diet, physical activity; weight loss is not effective without BED intervention.
Bariatric surgery requires long-term follow-up (BED preoperative assessment).
NICE NG69 — CBT or guided self-help first-line.
8. — FDA approval for BED; daily 30–70 mg.
Weight management and metabolic complications require long-term monitoring.
Myth 1: "BED is just obesity or a 'overeating' personality trait"
Clinical eating disorder; distinguishing features from binge eating episode (loss of control, shameful eating).
Restrictive diet triggers BED — weight control without psychological intervention increases binge eating.
Surgery reduces abdominal capacity but does not modify the cognitive-behavioral component; in some patients, a new pattern of "grazing" develops.
Myth 4: "Eating is an 'addiction' (food addiction), must be treated like drugs"
"Food addiction" is a concept but not accepted as a clinical diagnosis; standard eating disorder interventions are superior.
lisdexamfetamine FDA approval for BED; CBT first-line.
McElroy S.L. et al. Efficacy and safety of lisdexamfetamine for treatment of adults with moderate to severe binge eating disorder. JAMA Psychiatry 2015;72(3):235–246.
Wilson G.T., Wilfley D.E., Agras W.S., Bryson S.W. Psychological treatments of binge eating disorder. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2010;67(1):94–101.
